title: The Volcano and the Valley
bullets: Cuicuilco, the Basin of Mexico's largest city, was buried under 30 feet of lava when the Xitle volcano erupted around 50 BCE, sending thousands of refugees northeast to the Teotihuacan Valley.
The Teotihuacan Valley offered three decisive assets: reliable springs, fertile volcanic soil, and control of the Sierra de las Navajas and Otumba obsidian sources, the richest in central Mexico.
Obsidian was the most important industrial material in the pre-metal Americas, fracturing into blades sharper than surgical steel, and every civilization in Mesoamerica needed it.
The city's founders laid out a precise grid oriented 15.5 degrees east of true north, aligned to sunset on August 12 (the Long Count calendar starting date) and the Pleiades setting point.

The 15.5-degree grid was maintained for six centuries without deviation, implying either a centralized authority or a cultural tradition powerful enough to govern construction for twenty-plus generations.
The population surged from a few thousand to an estimated 20,000-40,000 within a generation of the Xitle eruption, providing the labor force that turned a cluster of villages into a planned city.

The Aztecs named the ruins Teotihuacan ('the place where the gods were born') 700 years after the city's collapse, but the original name, the language its people spoke, and the identity of every ruler remain unknown.

Teotihuacan's founding around 100 BCE coincides with the Roman Republic becoming an empire under Augustus, the Western Han dynasty in China, and the Olmec heartland cities already abandoned for centuries.

title: The Street of the Dead
bullets: The Pyramid of the Sun rises 216 feet with a base nearly as large as the Great Pyramid of Giza, containing an estimated 1.2 million cubic meters of earth and stone built in essentially a single construction campaign.

A natural cave beneath the Pyramid of the Sun was artificially enlarged into a clover-leaf shape; Mesoamerican creation myths placed humanity's origin in caves, making this the cosmological reason the pyramid was built here.

The Street of the Dead stretches 2 miles through the ceremonial core, its platforms progressively taller toward the north so that the Pyramid of the Moon appears to merge with the sacred mountain Cerro Gordo behind it.

Engineers diverted the San Juan River to flow along the 15.5-degree grid alignment, providing water and drainage for over 100,000 residents in a semi-arid valley receiving half the rainfall of the American Midwest.

The talud-tablero architectural style, a sloping base topped by a rectangular projecting panel, became so associated with Teotihuacan that its appearance at distant sites is considered direct evidence of the city's influence.
Lime plaster production required roughly three tons of wood per ton of plaster, potentially causing severe deforestation that some scholars link to the city's eventual environmental decline.

Around 100 CE, when the Pyramid of the Sun was being completed, Rome under Trajan was the capital of an empire of 60 million, yet both cities peaked in population simultaneously while knowing nothing of each other.
Teotihuacan achieved a planned urban grid rivaling Rome's without writing, metal tools, draft animals, or wheeled transport, using an entirely different technological toolkit.

title: The Pyramid of the Moon and the Feathered Serpent
bullets: The Pyramid of the Moon was enlarged seven times over 250 years, each phase accompanied by sacrificial burials of bound captives, exotic animals (jaguars, pumas, eagles, wolves), and hundreds of jade and obsidian offerings.
The Moon Pyramid's escalating sacrifices are the closest thing Teotihuacan has to a political history, each layer marking the state's growing power to capture enemies and import exotic materials.

The Temple of the Feathered Serpent in the Ciudadela compound featured carved serpent heads and Storm God faces covering its facades, with over 200 sacrificial victims buried beneath it during construction.

The 200 victims under the Feathered Serpent temple, many with hands bound and wearing necklaces of human jawbones, tripled the largest known sacrificial burial in Old World antiquity (the Royal Tombs of Ur at 74).

Around 250 CE the Temple of the Feathered Serpent was deliberately buried under the plain Adosada platform, interpreted as a political revolution where a new regime suppressed its predecessor's symbols.

After the Adosada revolution, Teotihuacan produced almost no art depicting identifiable individuals, suggesting power shifted from personal rule to collective governance, a council, or priestly oligarchy.

The Feathered Serpent deity, later known as Quetzalcoatl to the Aztecs and Kukulkan to the Maya, originated with the Olmec over a thousand years earlier; Teotihuacan's temple was its most dramatic architectural expression.
The city that built a monument to bury its own monument, replacing elaborate carved facades with blankness, expressed political change through architectural absence rather than assertion.

title: The Obsidian City
bullets: Over 400 obsidian workshops operated across the city, employing thousands of specialized knappers in mass production at a scale unmatched in the Americas until the industrial era.

Teotihuacan monopolized both green obsidian from the Sierra de las Navajas (the prestige variety) and gray obsidian from Otumba (for everyday tools), controlling extraction, production, and distribution across Mesoamerica.
The city's 2,000-plus apartment compounds each housed 60 to 100 people around shared courtyards with drainage systems, a uniform housing form found nowhere else in the ancient Americas.

Thin Orange pottery, produced in southern Puebla under Teotihuacan's control, functioned as the city's export porcelain, its distribution tracking Teotihuacan's sphere of commercial influence across Mesoamerica.

Mica sheets imported from over 1,800 miles away were laid beneath temple floors for unknown ritual purposes, invisible once installed, demonstrating the extraordinary reach of the city's trade networks.

Green obsidian production was concentrated near the ceremonial core under apparent state control, while gray obsidian workshops were scattered more freely, a two-tier economy with parallels to Roman mint-and-bakery distinction.
Jade and greenstone from Guatemala (600+ miles), cacao and rubber from the Gulf Coast, cinnabar from highland sources, and marine shells from both coasts all flowed into the Teotihuacan market.
Teotihuacan achieved urbanism without writing, coinage, wheeled transport, metal tools, or draft animals, solving the same problems the Old World addressed with entirely different technologies.

title: A City of Strangers
bullets: The Oaxacan Barrio housed Zapotec migrants from Monte Alban who maintained their own pottery styles, burial customs, and tomb architecture for centuries, a permanent multigenerational ethnic enclave inside a foreign city.

The Merchants' Barrio on the eastern side housed Gulf Coast traders who built round houses (unique in the rectangular grid), used Maya-style pottery, and maintained lowland cultural practices far from home.
Isotopic analysis of skeletal remains confirmed that many residents of ethnic barrios were first-generation immigrants, born elsewhere and migrated to Teotihuacan as adults.

At its peak around 400 CE, Teotihuacan held 125,000 to 200,000 people, the largest city in the Western Hemisphere by an enormous margin, a population no other American city would approach for a thousand years.

The average Teotihuacano ate maize tortillas and tamales supplemented by beans, squash, chili peppers, amaranth, and occasionally rabbit, turkey, or dog, with limited protein compared to Old World diets.
Households produced mass quantities of standardized ceramic figurines, mostly depicting women, for domestic religious rituals conducted in compound courtyard temples.

The ethnic barrios functioned as embedded trade networks: resident communities maintained connections to their homelands and channeled goods (cacao, rubber, feathers, jade, cochineal) into the Teotihuacan market.
Monte Alban and Teotihuacan maintained a diplomatic relationship for centuries, with carved stone monuments at Monte Alban depicting apparent Teotihuacan ambassadors visiting the Zapotec capital.

title: The Entrada
bullets: On January 16, 378 CE, a warrior named Siyaj K'ahk' arrived at Tikal, 630 miles from Teotihuacan, and the existing king died the same day, replaced by a Teotihuacan-backed dynasty within the year.

Everything we know about the Entrada comes from Maya inscriptions, not Teotihuacan records, the opposite of how conquest narratives usually work, with the victors adopting the conqueror's prestige as legitimacy.
Spearthrower Owl, recorded in Maya texts as the overlord who dispatched Siyaj K'ahk', may be one of the few Teotihuacan rulers whose name survives, though even this identification is uncertain.
The new Tikal dynasty dressed in Teotihuacan military regalia (goggle-eyed Storm God imagery, atlatl spear-throwers, war serpent headdresses) but wrote in Maya hieroglyphs, blending two traditions.

The highland Maya city of Kaminaljuyu had already adopted talud-tablero architecture and Teotihuacan burial practices, likely serving as a staging area for the 630-mile military expedition into the Peten.
In 426 CE, Copan's founder K'inich Yax K'uk' Mo' adopted Teotihuacan iconography; isotopic analysis showed he grew up in the Peten, but his authority derived partly from the Teotihuacan connection.

Teotihuacan did not conquer the Maya lowlands in the Roman sense; there was no occupation army, no provincial administration, no taxation system, just a single dramatic intervention followed by cultural influence.
The Entrada created a new political currency in the Maya world: claim a link to Teotihuacan and your dynasty gained an aura of legitimacy that purely Maya credentials could not provide.

We know the exact day of the Entrada more precisely than the day Julius Caesar crossed the Rubicon, thanks to the Maya Long Count calendar counting days from a mythological starting point in 3114 BCE.

title: The Great Goddess and the Storm God
bullets: Teotihuacan's religion is known entirely from its murals and sculpture; every deity name we use is borrowed from the Aztecs or assigned by modern scholars, since the city left no readable religious texts.

The Great Goddess appears at Tepantitla in a famous mural: a frontal figure with a bird headdress and jade nose bar, water flowing from her hands, presiding over a paradise where tiny figures play and sing.

The Storm God, recognizable by goggle eyes and fanged mouth, appears everywhere at Teotihuacan and was adopted by the Aztecs as Tlaloc; the visual continuity spanning a thousand years is not seriously disputed.

The Old God of Fire (later Huehueteotl), a hunched elderly figure bearing a fire bowl on his head, was one of the oldest continuously worshipped deities in Mesoamerica, likely a household rather than state deity.

Murals at Atetelco depict warriors in eagle and coyote costumes alongside jaguars devouring hearts, the clearest evidence of military orders that prefigured the Aztec eagle and jaguar warrior societies by a thousand years.

The atlatl (spear-thrower) was Teotihuacan's signature weapon, its depiction at distant sites like Tikal a direct reference to the city's military power, the equivalent of the Roman gladius or English longbow.

Teotihuacan's stone masks, carved from greenstone, basalt, and onyx with idealized features and no individual portraiture, embody the city's core principle: the erasure of the individual in favor of the collective.

Whether Teotihuacan chose not to develop writing or wrote on perishable materials that have not survived is debated; either way, the city's murals functioned as a state communication system, the walls as the library.

title: Fire
bullets: Around 550 CE the ceremonial center was systematically burned, targeting temples and elite buildings while leaving residential apartment compounds largely intact, a pattern consistent with internal revolt against the ruling elite.

Environmental stress from centuries of lime-plaster production may have caused severe deforestation, degrading agricultural land in a feedback loop: more building required more wood, which degraded more farmland.
After the fire the population dropped from over 100,000 to perhaps 20,000-40,000 within a generation, but the city was never completely abandoned; squatter settlements appeared among the ruins.
The Epiclassic period (550-900 CE) was defined by the power vacuum Teotihuacan left behind, with regional centers like Xochicalco, Cacaxtla, and Cholula competing to fill the void.

The Aztec Mexica arrived in the Basin of Mexico in the 13th century, made pilgrimages to the ruins, named the pyramids, and wove Teotihuacan into their creation mythology as the birthplace of the Fifth Sun.
In 1905 Leopoldo Batres excavated and botched the Pyramid of the Sun restoration, adding an inauthentic fifth terrace for Mexico's centennial celebration and destroying archaeological context.
Rene Millon's Mapping Project (1962-1973) revealed over 5,000 structures across 8 square miles of planned grid, transforming Teotihuacan from a ceremonial center into a recognized genuine city.

In 2003 a sealed tunnel beneath the Temple of the Feathered Serpent yielded liquid mercury, pyrite spheres, greenstone figurines, and glittering mineral-covered walls, possibly a ritual representation of the underworld.
Teotihuacan was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987 and receives over two million visitors annually; muon tomography and LiDAR continue revealing features the surface record missed.
